---
title: "View Introspection Network (VIN) on EVL"
format: html
---

# Scope

This page proposes (and documents) the initial **VIN** implementation for our project: a lightweight **RRI predictor head** trained on top of a **frozen EVL backbone** (EFM3D). It replaces expensive oracle computations at inference time by predicting **Relative Reconstruction Improvement (RRI)** for a set of candidate poses.

References:

- Paper summary: [VIN-NBV](../literature/vin_nbv.qmd)
- EFM3D/EVL implementation map: [EFM3D Implementation Index](../ext-impl/efm3d_implementation.qmd)
- External EVL docs: [Project Aria Tools — EVL](https://facebookresearch.github.io/projectaria_tools/docs/open_models/evl)
- External code: [facebookresearch/efm3d](https://github.com/facebookresearch/efm3d)
- CORAL ordinal regression (implementation): [coral-pytorch](https://raschka-research-group.github.io/coral-pytorch/) ([GitHub](https://github.com/Raschka-research-group/coral-pytorch))
- Oracle label pipeline: [RRI computation](rri_computation.qmd) and `oracle_rri/oracle_rri/pipelines/oracle_rri_labeler.py`

# Problem statement

Given:

- the current state encoded as a raw EFM snippet dict `efm: dict[str, Any]` (see `oracle_rri.data.EfmSnippetView.efm`), and
- a set of candidate next viewpoints as poses $T_{\mathrm{world}\leftarrow\mathrm{cam}_i}$ (`PoseTW`, **world←camera**) with $N$ candidates,

predict per-candidate scores $\widehat{\mathrm{RRI}}(q_i)$ such that selecting $\arg\max_i \widehat{\mathrm{RRI}}(q_i)$ approximates selecting $\arg\max_i \mathrm{RRI}(q_i)$ computed by the oracle.

# EVL backbone recap (what we tap into)

EVL (Egocentric Voxel Lifting) processes the snippet and produces a **voxel-aligned 3D representation**. Architecturally (simplified), it follows:

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    A["EFM snippet dict<br/>(images, poses, calib, semidense)"]
    A --> B["2D backbone<br/>(DinoV2 / video backbone)"]
    B --> C["Lifter<br/>(voxel feats + T_world_voxel)"]
    C --> D["3D neck<br/>(feature refinement)"]
    D --> E["Heads<br/>(occupancy + OBB detection)"]
    D --> F["VIN head<br/>(ours)"]
    F --> G["CORAL logits<br/>(ordinal RRI bins)"]
```

For VIN we want a compact, information-rich scene embedding that is (a) available for every snippet, (b) stable across training/inference, and (c) cheap to query for many candidates. This makes the **3D neck feature volumes** the best attachment point.

In `external/efm3d/efm3d/model/evl.py`, these are created right before the occupancy / OBB heads:

- `out["neck/occ_feat"] = neck_feats1`
- `out["neck/obb_feat"] = neck_feats2`

Our stable “feature contract” is implemented by `oracle_rri/oracle_rri/vin/backbone_evl.py` (`EvlBackbone`) and currently returns:

- `neck/occ_feat`: `Tensor["B Cocc D H W"]`
- `neck/obb_feat`: `Tensor["B Cobb D H W"]` (optional)
- `voxel/T_world_voxel`: `PoseTW["B 12"]` (world←voxel grid)
- `voxel_extent`: `Tensor["B 6"]` with `[$x_\min$,$x_\max$,$y_\min$,$y_\max$,$z_\min$,$z_\max$]` in voxel frame (metres)

# Candidate pose parameterization (shell-aware descriptor in reference frame)

VIN-NBV conditions the score on the candidate viewpoint **relative to the current state**. For ASE/EFM snippets, we define:

- reference pose: $T_{\mathrm{world}\leftarrow\mathrm{rig}_\mathrm{ref}}$ (last rig pose in the snippet),
- candidate pose: $T_{\mathrm{world}\leftarrow\mathrm{cam}_i}$ for candidate $i$.

The relative pose in the reference rig frame is:

$$
T_{\mathrm{rig}_\mathrm{ref}\leftarrow\mathrm{cam}_i}
=
T_{\mathrm{world}\leftarrow\mathrm{rig}_\mathrm{ref}}^{-1}\;
T_{\mathrm{world}\leftarrow\mathrm{cam}_i}.
$$

## Training-time alignment with `OracleRriLabeler` (preferred)

When training from oracle labels, we do **not** need to reconstruct the relative pose from world poses. The label pipeline already provides candidate poses in the reference frame:

- `oracle_rri_label_batch.depths.camera.T_camera_rig`: $T_{\mathrm{cam}\leftarrow\mathrm{rig}_\mathrm{ref}}$ (camera←rig, PoseTW)

We compute the descriptor from:

$$
T_{\mathrm{rig}_\mathrm{ref}\leftarrow\mathrm{cam}} = T_{\mathrm{cam}\leftarrow\mathrm{rig}_\mathrm{ref}}^{-1}.
$$

## Shell descriptor $(r,u,f,\text{scalars})$

Let $T_{\mathrm{rig}\leftarrow\mathrm{cam}} = (R_{\mathrm{rig}\leftarrow\mathrm{cam}}, t_{\mathrm{rig}\leftarrow\mathrm{cam}})$. We define:

- radius: $r = \lVert t \rVert$,
- position direction: $u = t / (r + \varepsilon) \in \mathbb{S}^2$,
- forward direction: $f = R_{\mathrm{rig}\leftarrow\mathrm{cam}}\;z_{\mathrm{cam}} \in \mathbb{S}^2$, with $z_{\mathrm{cam}}=(0,0,1)$ (LUF camera convention),
- simple scalar terms, e.g. $\langle f, -u \rangle$ (“looks back towards reference center”).

This descriptor matches our candidate sampling prior (shell around a reference pose), and avoids using $\log(\mathrm{SO}(3))$ or any `so3log` mapping.

# Pose encoding: spherical harmonics (directions) + 1D Fourier (radius)

We encode the two unit vectors $u,f \in \mathbb{S}^2$ using **real spherical harmonics** up to degree $L$ computed with **e3nn**.

- Feature dimension of the SH basis is $(L+1)^2$.
- Implementation: `oracle_rri/oracle_rri/vin/spherical_encoding.py` (`ShellShPoseEncoder`) via `e3nn.o3.spherical_harmonics`.

For the radius, **SH is not the right basis** (it is a basis on $\mathbb{S}^2$, not on $\mathbb{R}$). We therefore use **1D Fourier features** for $r$:

- encode either $r$ or $\log(r+\varepsilon)$ (default: $\log(r+\varepsilon)$),
- apply learnable Fourier features (sin/cos) and project to a learnable embedding.

Concretely, for a scalar input $s \in \mathbb{R}$ we use:

$$
\phi(s) = \big[s,\;\sin(2\pi B s),\;\cos(2\pi B s)\big], \quad B \in \mathbb{R}^{F\times 1}.
$$

# VIN head (current baseline + planned v0.2 upgrade)

`oracle_rri/oracle_rri/vin/model.py` (`VinModel`) implements a lightweight scorer head on top of frozen EVL:

1. Run frozen EVL once on the snippet to get voxel features + voxel grid pose.
2. For each candidate:
   - pose embedding: shell descriptor encoded via SH($u,f$) + 1D Fourier ($r$),
   - local voxel embedding: sample EVL neck features at the candidate camera center,
   - global voxel embedding: global mean+max pooling of EVL neck features.
3. Concatenate embeddings and predict ordinal logits (CORAL) via an MLP + `CoralLayer`.

## Candidate-conditioned voxel query (TODO)

Sampling features only at the camera center is a weak view descriptor. The planned upgrade is a **frustum point query**:

- sample $K$ points in front of the camera along a small set of rays and depths,
- transform points to world via $T_{\mathrm{world}\leftarrow\mathrm{cam}}$,
- map to voxel coordinates via $T_{\mathrm{voxel}\leftarrow\mathrm{world}} = T_{\mathrm{world}\leftarrow\mathrm{voxel}}^{-1}$,
- sample voxel features at those points and pool (mean+max), masking invalid points.

# Training objective (VIN-NBV style): CORAL ordinal regression

VIN-NBV reports that directly regressing RRI is difficult; it discretizes RRI into ordinal bins and trains with CORAL.

We follow the same structure:

1. **Oracle labels**: for each snippet, generate candidates and compute oracle RRI using `OracleRriLabeler` (candidate → render depth → backproject PCs → mesh distance → RRI).
2. **Binning**: convert RRI to ordinal labels $y\in\{0,\dots,K-1\}$ using `oracle_rri/oracle_rri/vin/rri_binning.py` (`RriOrdinalBinner`). The current baseline uses a single stage id (0) and quantile edges after optional $\tanh$ clipping.
3. **Loss**: CORAL loss (sum of BCEs over ordinal thresholds) using `oracle_rri/oracle_rri/vin/coral.py` (wraps `coral-pytorch`).

At inference time we use the expected ordinal value (normalized to $[0,1]$) as the candidate score.

# Minimal training script

A minimal end-to-end training smoke test is provided at `oracle_rri/scripts/train_vin.py`. It does:

- load a real ASE snippet (local ATEK shards + meshes),
- run `OracleRriLabeler` online to produce labels,
- fit a binner (few snippets),
- train the head (EVL frozen) for a small number of steps.

Example:

```bash
cd oracle_rri
uv run python scripts/train_vin.py --max-steps 5 --fit-snippets 2 --max-candidates 8 --device auto
```

# Implementation map

VIN code lives in `oracle_rri/oracle_rri/vin/`:

- `backbone_evl.py`: `EvlBackbone` wrapper that consumes raw EFM dicts and returns `neck/*` volumes + voxel pose/extent.
- `spherical_encoding.py`: `ShellShPoseEncoder` (e3nn SH for $u,f$ + 1D Fourier features for radius).
- `pose_encoding.py`: Learnable Fourier features baseline modules (as per Learnable Fourier Features, NeurIPS 2021).
- `model.py`: `VinModel` (pose encoding + voxel querying + MLP + CORAL).
- `coral.py`: CORAL layer + loss and probability utilities (via `coral-pytorch`).
- `rri_binning.py`: RRI→ordinal binning (`RriOrdinalBinner`).

# Known gaps / next steps (tracked in `todos.qmd`)

- implement candidate frustum query + strict point-level validity masking,
- add ranking metrics (Spearman, top-$k$ recall) to the training loop,
- version bin thresholds by candidate generator config + EVL ckpt id,
- run ablations: occ-only vs occ+obb, $L=2$ vs $L=3$, query depths/rays.
